translations.translation:
Xu Huang (Gongming)
徐晃 (公明)
Lived ADca.169–227
aSanguozhiBiographyRecords of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]
Scroll 17 (Wei 17)
byZL181
onMarch 11th, 2025

Xu Huang, styledGongming, was fromYang[楊],Hedong[河東].
He served as an official for his commandery. He followed theGeneral of Chariots and Cavalry,Yang Feng, in suppressing traitors. Rendering achievements, he was conferred asCavalry Commandant. WhenLi JueandGuo Sicaused chaos inChang’an[長安],[Xu] Huangtold[Yang] Fengto return toLuoyang[洛陽] with theSon of Heaven.[Yang] Fengheeded his plan. TheSon of Heavenferried across theYellow River[河] toAnyi[安邑] and enfeoffed[Xu] HuangasMarquis of a Capital District. When they arrived atLuoyang[洛陽],Han XianandDong Chengstruggled with each other every day, so[Xu] Huangtold[Yang] Fengto submit to theGrand Progenitor.[Yang] Fenghad wanted to heeded him, but he regretted it later. When theGrand Progenitorsuppressed[Yang] FengatLiang[梁],[Xu] Huangjoined theGrand Progenitor. TheGrand Progenitorgave[Xu] Huangtroops and sent him to attack the rebels atJuan[卷] andYuanwu[原武].ABecause he defeated them, he was conferred asLieutenant General.
He followed in the expedition againstLü Bu, separating to gain the surrenders of the generals of[Lü] Bu,Zhao ShuandLi Zou, among others. He andShi HuanbeheadedSui GuatHenei[河內]. He also followed to defeatLiu Bei, and he also followed to defeatYan Liang. He capturedBaima[白馬], advanced toYan ford[延津], and he defeatedWen Chou, and was conferred asDeputy General. He andCao Hongstruck a rebel atYinqiang[㶏強],Zhu Bi, and defeated him. He andShi Huanthen struck the army supplies ofYuan ShaoatGushi[故市], their achievements vast. He was enfeoffed asMarquis of a Capital District.
Juan [卷] has the fanqie of Xu [墟] and Quan [權].
When theGrand ProgenitorbesiegedYe[鄴], he defeatedHandan. ThePrefectofYiyang[易陽令],Han Fan, pretended to surrender his city, then defended it with resistance. TheGrand Progenitorsent[Xu] Huangto attack him. When[Xu] Huangarrived, he shot an arrow into the city [with a letter], explaining success and defeat for him.[Han] Fantherefore had regrets and[Xu] Huanggot his surrender. He then informed this to theGrand Progenitor, saying, “The two Yuans have not yet been defeated and the various cities that have not capitulated are straining their ears to listen to us. Today, if we obliterateYiyang[易陽], the next day, every one of them would defend to the death. I fear that there would then be no date that theHebei Region[河北] would be stabilized in. I hope thatYour Excellencywill accept the surrender ofYiyang[易陽] publicly to the cities so that there will be none of them that will not look up to you.” TheGrand Progenitorregarded this as good.
He separated to invadeMaocheng[毛城] and set up ambush troops to attack suddenly, thus defeating three garrisons. He followed to defeatYuan TanatNanpi[南皮], then suppressed the rebelling traitors atPingyuan[平原] and overcame them. He followed in the expedition againstTadunand was conferred to beGeneral who Clears the Wilderness. He followed in the expedition againstJing province[荆州] and separated to garrison atFan[樊], then suppressed the traitors ofZhonglu[中廬],Linju[臨沮], andYicheng[宜城]. He andMan ChongsuppressedGuan YuatHanjin[漢津], and he andCao RenattackedZhou YuatJiangling[江陵]. In the fifteenth year [of theera] [210], he went to suppress the rebels ofTaiyuan[太原], besiegingDaling[大陵] and capturing it, beheading the rebel leaderShang Yao.
WhenHan SuiandMa Chao, among others, rebelled in theGuanyou region[關右],[Xu] Huangwas sent to garrisonWanrong[汾陰] so as to swayHedong[河東]. He was gifted oxen and wine so he could present it to the graves of his ancestors. When theGrand ProgenitorreachedTong Pass[潼關], he feared he could not ferry across [Puban ford[蒲坂津]], so he summoned and consulted[Xu] Huang.[Xu] Huangsaid, “Your Excellency, you have amassed troops here, and yet the traitors have not put up a defense atPuban[蒲阪]. This is how you know they have no plan. Now if you would lend this ministerBsome elite troops to ferry acrossPuban ford[蒲坂津], I would be ahead of your army and obstruct their inner areas. The traitors can then be captured.” TheGrand Progenitorsaid, “Very well!” He sent[Xu] Huangwith four thousand infantry and cavalry to ferry across the ford. He had not finished making trenches and fences when the traitorLiang Xingled over five thousand infantry and cavalry overnight to attack[Xu] Huang, but[Xu] Huangstruck him and routed him. The army of theGrand Progenitorthus ferried across, and defeated[Ma] Chaoand the others.[Xu] HuangandXiahou Yuanpacified the various Di ofYumi[隃麋] andQian[汧諸], and they and theGrand Progenitormet up atAnding[安定].
Your minister, [Pei] Songzhi, would comment on this.[Xu] Huangwould not yet have called himself a minister at this time. What this biography wrote iserroneous.1•••
Cao Caowas not yet a duke or a king at this time, he was technically on the same level as any otherHan dynastyminister. Chen Shou may have wrote “this minister” as an accident, orXu Huangreally did say it, in which the implications can be deduced.↩
When theGrand Progenitorreturned toYe, he had[Xu] HuangandXiahou Yuanpacify the remaining traitors ofFuandXiayang, and they beheadedLiang Xingand received the surrender of over three thousand households. They followed in an expedition againstZhang Lu.[Xu] Huangwould then be sent separately to suppress the mountain Di of Gongdu and Chouyi, and he defeated them all. He was promoted toGeneral who Pacifies Enemies. He would relieve the siege ofGeneralGuanShun. He attacked thirty garrisons of the traitorChen Fuand others, and he defeated them all.
When theGrand Progenitorreturned toYe, he had[Xu] HuangandXiahou YuanresistLiu BeiatYangping.[Liu] BeisentChen Shiand some others with over ten camps to cut off Maming plank bridge,2but[Xu] Huangdetached to suppress and defeated them. The traitors threw themselves into the mountain valleys, and many of them died. When theGrand Progenitorheard of this, he was very pleased, and he gave[Xu] Huangacting tally. His order read, “These plank bridges are essential areas and chokepoints inHanzhong, andLiu Beiwanted to cut them off entirely so that he could seizeHanzhong. You,General, in one action, overcame the plan of the traitors, which is the greatest of excellence.” TheGrand Progenitorthen arrived atYangpingand pulled back the armies ofHanzhong.•••
This type of bridge would have been built next to a mountain for troops to move through.↩
[Xu] Huangwas then sent to aidCao Renin suppressingGuan Yu. He would garrison atYuan. It happened that theHan Riversurged and overflowed, andYu Jin, among others, were submerged.[Guan] Yusieged[Cao] RenatFan, and he also sieged GeneralLü ChangatXiangyang. Many of the people[Xu] Huangled were fresh soldiers, and because it was hard to struggle with[Guan] Yu, he advanced to Yangling pond to garrison. When theGrand Progenitorreturned again, he sent GeneralsXu ShangandLü Jian, among others, to[Xu] Huang. The order read, “Await the arrival of soldiers and horses, then advance together.” The traitors were garrisoned at Yancheng. When[Xu] Huangcame, he used the way of deceit to create a large trench, appearing as though they intended to cut off their rear. The traitors burnt their garrisons and fled, and[Xu] Huangthus got Yancheng. He set up two camps from each other and gradually advanced. He would be threezhang3away from where the siege by the traitors was. He had not attacked when theGrand ProgenitorsentYin ShuandZhu Gai, among others, with twelve camps to[Xu] Huang. There was a garrison at the main part of the siege by the traitors, and there was a separate garrison at the four mounds.[Xu] Huangmade it known that he would attack the garrison at the main part of the siege, but he secretly attacked the four mounds.[Guan] Yusaw that the four mounds [garrison] was being destroyed, so he led five thousand infantry and cavalry to fight, but[Xu] Huangstruck him and defeated him and caused him to flee. He then pursued them and breached, entering the siege with them. He then defeated them, and some of them threw themselves into theMian riverand died. The order of theGrand Progenitorstated, “The enemy siege dug ten layers of deer-antler trenches,4but theGeneralbrought about a complete victory in the fight, thus breaching the siege of the enemy and beheading many enemies. I have been utilizing troops for over thirty years, and of the excellent ways of using troops in the past that I have heard about, never was there one about charging into the siege of the foe. Moreover, the sieges atFanandXiangyangwere worse than the ones at Ju and Jimo,5thus the achievements of theGeneralsurpassSun Wu6and [Sima] Rangju.7”[Xu] Huangorganized his troops and returned toMobei, but theGrand Progenitorwelcomed[Xu] Huangsevenliaway and set up a great wine party. TheGrand Progenitorraised wine cups to encourage[Xu] Huang, and further expressed gratitude, saying, “Everything inFanandXiangyangwas achievements of yours,General.” At the time, the armies were all gathered. When theGrand Progenitorpatrolled the various camps, the soldiers were all disorganized in their formations, but the camp of[Xu] Huangwas organized and uniform, with its generals and officers staying in formation and not moving. TheGrand Progenitorsighed and said, "General Xucan be said to have the demeanor of Zhou Yafu.8”•••
If onezhangis about 333.33 centimeters,Xu Huangwould have been ten meters or about eleven yards away from the siege.↩
These were formations where sharp pieces of wood were placed at the rim of the camp, facing outward in order to stop the enemy. The overall formation looked like a deer antler, hence the name.↩
In the Warring States period, the State of Qi suffered a huge invasion, where the majority of its cities were lost. It is said that they only had two cities at the nadir of the war, but the talented general, Tian Dan, quickly pushed back the invaders with ingenious plans.↩
Sun Wuis credited with authoring theArt of War, a work thatCao Caogreatly admired and had many “excellent ways of using troops” thatCao Caooften used.↩
Sima Rangju was a talented military general who was good at counterattacking enemies. He, likeSun Wu, wrote a book on war, whichCao Caowould have been familiar with.↩
Zhou Yafu was a general of theHan dynasty. When the emperor toured his camp, the troops were all organized and disciplined strictly. He would serve as a highly successful general and put down a significant rebellion.↩
After theCivil Emperorbecame King, he had[Xu] Huangserve asGeneral of the Rightand advanced the fief toTownship Marquisof Lu, and after he ascended the throne steps, he advanced the fief toMarquisof Yang. He andXiahou ShangsuppressedLiu BeiatShangyongand defeated him. Because[Xu] HuangdefendedYangping, he shifted fief toMarquisofYangping. After theBrightEmperorascended the throne, he resisted theWugeneralZhuge JinatXiangyang. His fief was increased by two hundred households, joining with the previous for a total of three thousand and one hundred households. When he was severely ill, he made his last wishes to be buried in the clothes he normally wore.
***
By nature, he was frugal and economical, and he was vigilant and cautious. When leading troops, he often had scouts far from him. He would first make himself to be unbeatable, then he would fight. He would pursue fleeing [enemies] and strive for advantages9such that his soldiers had no rest for eating. He often sighed, “The ancients detested not meeting a wise lord, but now that I have been lucky to come across one, I should constantly work hard by making achievements; how can I act for my personal reputation?” He ultimately did not expand hisrelationships.•••
Xu Huangtried his best to fight when he had the advantage or seize the advantage during battle.↩
***
In the first year of theera [227], he died. His posthumous title was theRobustMarquis. His son,[Xu] Gai, succeeded him. When[Xu] Gaidied, his son,[Xu] Ba, succeeded him. TheBrightEmperordivided the fief of[Xu] Huangto enfeoff a son and a grandson of[Xu] Huangas ranged marquises.
***
Previously,Zhu LingofQingheserved as a general forYuan Shao. When theGrand Progenitorwas on an expedition againstTao Qian,[Yuan] Shaosent[Zhu] Lingto supervise three battalions to assist theGrand Progenitor, and rendered achievements in battle. The generals that[Yuan] Shaosent were each dismissed and they each returned.[Zhu] Lingsaid, “I have observed many a person: none of them can compare toExcellency Cao. He is truly a wise lord. Now that I have met him, where else can I go?” So he remained and did not leave. The soldiers that he led admired this, so they all followed[Zhu] Lingin remaining.[Zhu] Linglater became a good general and his reputation was second to[Xu] Huangand the others. He would attain the rank ofGeneral of the Rearand was enfeoffed asDistrict Marquis10ofGaotang.C•••
District Marquisshould beMarquishere, this is an erroneous error byChen Shou.Gaotangwas a county, which was associated with the title ofMarquis.↩
TheChronicles of the Nine Provincesstates: Previously,Ji YongofQinghehadShu[鄃] rebel againstYuan Shaoand surrender toGongsun Zan.[Gongsun] Zansent troops to defend him while[Yuan] Shaosent[Zhu] Lingto attack them. The family of[Zhu] Lingwas inside the city, and[Gongsun] Zanled the mother and younger brother of[Zhu] Lingonto the city and called to entice[Zhu] Ling.[Zhu] Linglooked at the city and shed tears, saying, “Once a man leaves for someone else, how can he look back towards his family?” He thus fought hard and captured it, capturing[Ji] Yongalive. However, the family of[Zhu] Lingall perished.
TheBook of Weistates:[Zhu] Lingwas styledWenbo. When theGrand ProgenitorpacifiedJi province, he sent[Zhu] Lingto lead five thousand new soldiers and one thousand cavalry to guard south ofXu. TheGrand Progenitorwarned him, saying, “These new soldiers ofJi provincehad often received lenience and laxness. If they suddenly are organized into an orderly fashion, they would be sullen. You have a reputation of being authoritative and strict, but you ought to be good at showing leniency by some method, lest there be problematic developments. When[Zhu] Lingarrived atYangzhai,Household GeneralCheng Angand others indeed rebelled, so he beheaded[Cheng] Angand reported it. TheGrand Progenitorwrote a letter to him, saying:
“The reason why being in an army is dangerous is because they are facing off against enemy states on the outside while having treacherous plots that will unleash volatility in the inside. In the past, whenDeng Yupartitioned the army of theGlorious and Martial[Emperor]while marching west, the conundrum of Zong Xin and Feng Yin occurred. Later on, he led twenty-four cavalrymen back toLuoyang. How could[Deng] Yuhave been ruined by those? Your letter is candid and contrite, and you have cited many errors, but I am not yet sure if it is as you claim.11”•••
In the past,Deng Yuwas put in charge of a military expedition to the west. This is presumably the outside thatCao Caois talking about.Deng Yuformed a military group that contained his subordinates Zong Xin and Feng Yin. However, they fought each other and Feng Yin killed Zong Xin, then rebelled. This is presumably the inside thatCao Caois talking about.Deng Yulater took care of Feng Yin. Later on, he suffered a huge defeat (unrelated to Feng Yin) such that he only had twenty-four cavalrymen after the battle. He blamed himself and resigned from his titles, though he was shown mercy. Despite all of this, his achievements in military and political matters allowed for the Eastern Han dynasty to begin with a strong start. Essentially,Cao Caothinks thatZhu Lingshould not blame himself.↩
After theCivil Emperorassumed the throne, he enfeoffed[Zhu] LingasMarquisofShu[鄃] and increased his fief and households. The edict stated:
“General, you have aided thedeparted emperorand led troops for several years. Your might exceeds Fang12and Shao13and your merits surpasses Jiang14and Guan.15How can praises of you in books and records get even better? I received theMandate of Heavenand I reign paramount over thelands within the Seas. Generals of supreme achievements and ministers of theAltars of Statewill all share my fortune and my felicity, and I will pass them down without ever an end. I now enfeoff you asMarquisofShu[鄃]. If you do not return to your former village when wealthy and honored, it would be like walking in the night and wearing extravagant clothing.16Supposing if you have mundane ambitions, however, I hope you will not find it hard to saythem.”•••
Fang refers to Fangshu, who was a successful military general who expandedZhouterritory in the region aroundJing province.↩
Shao refers to Duke Shao Hu, who was one of the regents of the Gonghe Regency. He was also a capable military general who won a battle despite being heavily outnumbered.↩
Jiang refers to Zhou Bo, the Marquis of Jiang. His many military victories helped consolidate theHanin its earliest stage.↩
Guan refers to Guan Ying. His many military victories helped strengthen theHan.↩
This is a quote from Xiang Yu. He said it as a way to show off his power: he wanted his former neighbors to know that he had attained power. Otherwise, no one will know.↩
[Zhu] Lingexpressed his gratitude and said, "Gaotangis what I longed for.” Thus, his fief was changed toMarquisofGaotang. When he died, his posthumous title was theMightyMarquis. His son,[Zhu] Shu, succeededhim.17•••
In some versions of this biography, the phrase “his son,[Zhu] Shu, succeeded him” [子術嗣] is missing, and it is not known why.↩
ㅤ
